model_narrative: This is a large agricultural-water-resource planning model for the Indus basin, written in fully expanded coefficient-matrix form. There is one continuous decision variable for each column of the model, each kept within its own lower and upper bound. The model is driven by a sparse coefficient matrix together with a right-hand side and a sense for every row. The objective is to choose the variable levels so as to maximize the total consumer-plus-producer surplus, computed as each variable's level weighted by its objective coefficient, plus a fixed constant.

sets.name: V
sets.doc: the indices of the decision variables, one per column of the model

sets.name: R
sets.doc: the indices of the constraint rows of the model, one per relationship to enforce

params.name: A
params.doc: the sparse coefficient matrix; A[r, v] is the coefficient of variable v in row r, and entries that are absent are zero, so only the variables that actually appear in a row contribute to it

params.name: rhs
params.doc: the right-hand side constant of each constraint row

params.name: csense
params.doc: the sense of each constraint row given as a single letter: 'E' means the row is an equality, 'L' means the row's weighted sum is bounded above by its right-hand side, and 'G' means it is bounded below

params.name: objc
params.doc: the objective coefficient of each variable, its per-unit contribution to surplus

params.name: obj_const
params.doc: a constant term added to the objective

params.name: lb
params.doc: the lower bound of each variable, zero unless overridden

params.name: ub
params.doc: the upper bound of each variable, unbounded above unless overridden

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the level chosen for each decision variable, bounded between its lower and upper bound

constraints.name: A.keys()
constraints.intent: Every row of the model imposes one relationship between a weighted combination of the decision variables and a fixed right-hand side. For each row, form the weighted sum of the variables that appear in that row, each multiplied by its coefficient in that row, and require that this sum relates to the row's right-hand side according to the row's own sense. When the sense marks the row as an equality the weighted sum must equal the right-hand side, when it marks the row as an upper-bound type the weighted sum must not exceed the right-hand side, and when it marks the row as a lower-bound type the weighted sum must be at least the right-hand side.

query: The plan leans hard on the four big aggregate accounts that net out as x2, x4, x6 and x8 — together they're carrying north of 41000 units of throughput. There's a proposal on the table to put a single combined ceiling across those four accounts, holding their total to 30000. What does the optimizer return for total surplus once that joint cap is in place?

query: Right now activity x8 makes up close to a quarter of the combined x4-plus-x8 account. Engineering is pitching a concentration rule that would hold x8 to no more than 20 percent of the sum of x4 and x8 taken together. Plug that in and walk me through what total surplus comes out to.

query: We're looking at a uniform per-line ceiling across the block of capped seasonal activities running from x1263 through x1322 — every one of those individual lines would be held to at most 120 units rather than leaving each free up to its own limit. What does the optimizer return for total surplus under that flat per-activity cap?

query: There's a two-part scenario the board wants evaluated as a single package: first, hold the combined throughput of the x2, x4, x6 and x8 accounts to 38000, and on top of that keep x8 under 18 percent of the sum of x4 and x8 together. Apply both at once and tell me where total surplus ends up.
expected_pyomo:
model.load_ceiling = Constraint(expr=model.x['x2'] + model.x['x4'] + model.x['x6'] + model.x['x8'] <= 38000)
model.x8_share = Constraint(expr=model.x['x8'] <= 0.18 * (model.x['x4'] + model.x['x8']))

query: The block of capped seasonal activities x1263 through x1274 is soaking up well over 2700 units between them, and that whole corridor feels overcommitted. Couldn't we just hold their combined total under 1000 and free up room elsewhere? What's the model seeing in that corridor that I'm not?

query: The x4 account is running at more than three times the size of the x8 account, which looks lopsided to me. Shouldn't x4 at least be held no larger than x8 so the two stay in step? What's pulling the optimizer toward letting x4 run so far ahead?

query: Looking at the x6 and x8 accounts side by side, x6 is carrying almost half of their combined load. Wouldn't it be obvious to keep x6 down to at most 40 percent of the sum of x6 and x8 together so neither one dominates? What's the tradeoff the optimizer is making by tilting it toward x6?

query: A handful of the high-volume routing lines — x1722, x1723, x1724, x1729, x1752, x1753 and x1777 — are each running thousands of units, with one of them up past 20000. Couldn't we just hold every one of those lines to 3000 apiece and even the flow out? What's the model getting out of letting a few of them run so hot?
